2606.28186.title: Cognitive Episodes in LLM Reasoning Traces Enable Interpretable Human Item Difficulty Prediction
2606.28186.paper_summary: Predicting human item difficulty is central to educational assessment, where reliable estimates support fairness and effective test construction. Existing methods often depend on costly human calibration or item-level textual representations, providing limited evidence about the cognitive processes that make items difficult. We argue that difficulty should be viewed not only as a property of item text, but also as an observable consequence of the problem-solving burden an item induces. Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) offer scalable process evidence through reasoning traces, but such evidence must be structured to support interpretable modeling. To this end, we introduce Epi2Diff (Episode to Difficulty), a framework that maps LRM reasoning traces into cognitively grounded episode sequences. These episodes group trace segments into functional problem-solving states, enabling difficulty to be modeled through reasoning scale, effort allocation, and state transitions. Epi2Diff extracts compact episode-dynamic features and combines them with semantic item representations for human difficulty prediction. Experiments on four real-world human difficulty datasets show that Epi2Diff consistently outperforms strong baselines, including fine-tuned small language models, LLM in-context learning, and supervised LLM adaptation. On SAT-derived classification benchmarks, Epi2Diff achieves an 8.1% average relative gain over supervised LLM fine-tuning baselines. Further analyses show that harder items induce more effortful, iterative, and implementation-centered episode dynamics, rather than merely longer responses. These results demonstrate that cognitive episodes in LRM reasoning traces provide a predictive and interpretable process representation for human item difficulty, offering a new lens for educational measurement with reasoning models.
2606.28186.paper_summary_zh: 預測人類項目難度是教育評估的核心，可靠的估計支持公平和有效的測試構建。現有的方法通常依賴於昂貴的人為校準或項目級的文本表示，提供關於使項目變得困難的認知過程的有限證據。我們認為，難度不僅應被視為項目文本的一個屬性，還應被視為項目所引發的問題解決負擔的可觀察後果。大型推理模型（LRMs）通過推理痕跡提供可擴展的過程證據，但這些證據必須結構化以支持可解釋的建模。為此，我們介紹了Epi2Diff（從情節到難度），這是一個將LRM推理痕跡映射到認知基礎情節序列的框架。這些情節將痕跡片段分組為功能性問題解決狀態，使得難度可以通過推理規模、努力分配和狀態轉換來建模。Epi2Diff提取緊湊的情節動態特徵，並將其與語義項目表示結合，用於人類難度預測。在四個真實世界的人類難度數據集上的實驗顯示，Epi2Diff始終優於強基準，包括微調的小型語言模型、LLM的上下文學習和監督式LLM適應。在基於SAT的分類基準上，Epi2Diff相對於監督式LLM微調基準實現了8.1%的平均相對增益。進一步的分析顯示，更難的項目引發了更費力的、迭代的和以實施為中心的情節動態，而不僅僅是更長的回應。這些結果表明，LRM推理痕跡中的認知情節為人類項目難度提供了可預測和可解釋的過程表示，為使用推理模型的教育測量提供了一個新的視角。
2606.28186.author: Chenguang Wang et.al.
2606.28186.authors: Chenguang Wang, Ming Li, Xinyue Zeng, Zhuochun Li, Hong Jiao, Tianyi Zhou, Dawei Zhou
